title: Longest Palindromic Substring
description: Given s, return the longest palindromic substring.

examples.explanation:
String length: 5
Initialize longest = ""

i=0:
  j=0: substring="b", palindrome=true, longest="b"
  j=1: substring="ba", palindrome=false
  j=2: substring="bab", palindrome=true, length=3 > 1, longest="bab"
  j=3: substring="baba", palindrome=false
  j=4: substring="babad", palindrome=false

i=1:
  j=1: substring="a", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=2: substring="ab", palindrome=false
  j=3: substring="aba", palindrome=true, length=3 = 3, keep "bab"
  j=4: substring="abad", palindrome=false

i=2:
  j=2: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=3: substring="ba", palindrome=false
  j=4: substring="bad", palindrome=false

i=3:
  j=3: substring="a", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=4: substring="ad", palindrome=false

i=4:
  j=4: substring="d", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3

Final longest = "bab"

String length: 4
Initialize longest = ""

i=0:
  j=0: substring="c", palindrome=true, longest="c"
  j=1: substring="cb", palindrome=false
  j=2: substring="cbb", palindrome=false
  j=3: substring="cbbd", palindrome=false

i=1:
  j=1: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 1
  j=2: substring="bb", palindrome=true, length=2 > 1, longest="bb"
  j=3: substring="bbd", palindrome=false

i=2:
  j=2: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 2
  j=3: substring="bd", palindrome=false

i=3:
  j=3: substring="d", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 2

Final longest = "bb"

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check all possible substrings for palindromes by verifying each from both ends. Use nested loops to generate substrings and a helper function to check palindrome property.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution for Longest Palindromic Substring
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible substrings for palindrome property using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach solves the longest palindromic substring problem by systematically examining every possible substring of the input string. The intuition is straightforward: if we check every substring, we're guaranteed to find the longest palindrome, though this comes at significant computational cost. The algorithm works by using two nested loops to generate all possible substrings (i to j where i ≤ j), then for each substring, it checks if it's a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends inward. The palindrome check itself requires O(k) time where k is the substring length, leading to O(n³) total time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) since we only store the current best palindrome and indices. This connects directly to the problem statement as it provides a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in substring indices, forgetting to handle edge cases like empty strings or single characters, and inefficient palindrome checking that doesn't stop early when mismatch is found. The real pattern involves careful index management and early termination conditions.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize longest = ""
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
3.   For j from i to n-1:
4.     If substring s[i:j+1] is palindrome AND length > longest.length:
5.       Update longest = s[i:j+1]
6. Return longest

Time: O(n³) - Three nested operations: outer loop O(n), inner loop O(n), palindrome check O(n)
Space: O(1) - Only storing the result string

examples.trace:
String length: 5
Initialize longest = ""

i=0:
  j=0: substring="b", palindrome=true, longest="b"
  j=1: substring="ba", palindrome=false
  j=2: substring="bab", palindrome=true, length=3 > 1, longest="bab"
  j=3: substring="baba", palindrome=false
  j=4: substring="babad", palindrome=false

i=1:
  j=1: substring="a", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=2: substring="ab", palindrome=false
  j=3: substring="aba", palindrome=true, length=3 = 3, keep "bab"
  j=4: substring="abad", palindrome=false

i=2:
  j=2: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=3: substring="ba", palindrome=false
  j=4: substring="bad", palindrome=false

i=3:
  j=3: substring="a", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3
  j=4: substring="ad", palindrome=false

i=4:
  j=4: substring="d", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 3

Final longest = "bab"

String length: 4
Initialize longest = ""

i=0:
  j=0: substring="c", palindrome=true, longest="c"
  j=1: substring="cb", palindrome=false
  j=2: substring="cbb", palindrome=false
  j=3: substring="cbbd", palindrome=false

i=1:
  j=1: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 1
  j=2: substring="bb", palindrome=true, length=2 > 1, longest="bb"
  j=3: substring="bbd", palindrome=false

i=2:
  j=2: substring="b", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 2
  j=3: substring="bd", palindrome=false

i=3:
  j=3: substring="d", palindrome=true, length=1 ≤ 2

Final longest = "bb"

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Checker Function
ladders.desc: Function that determines if a given string is a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends.
ladders.explanation:
The palindrome checker is the core validation function in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: a string is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backwards. This is verified by comparing the first character with the last, the second with the second-last, and so on, moving inward from both ends. The algorithm uses two pointers (left and right) that start at the string's extremes and converge toward the center. At each step, if characters at these positions don't match, the function immediately returns false. If all comparisons succeed, the string is a palindrome. This connects to L0 as it's called for every substring generated by the nested loops. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the string length, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include incorrect index calculations, not handling empty strings properly, and continuing comparisons after finding a mismatch. The real pattern involves clean pointer movement and early termination.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0, right = len(s)-1
2. While left < right:
3.   If s[left] ≠ s[right]: return false
4.   left++, right--
5. Return true

Time: O(k) where k is input string length
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Input: "racecar" (length 7)
left=0, right=6: compare 'r' and 'r' → match
left=1, right=5: compare 'a' and 'a' → match
left=2, right=4: compare 'c' and 'c' → match
left=3, right=3: left < right is false, loop ends
All comparisons matched → return true

Input: "hello" (length 5)
left=0, right=4: compare 'h' and 'o' → mismatch
Immediately return false

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Substrings with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Technique to systematically generate every possible substring of a string using two nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central mechanism of the brute force approach: generating all possible substrings. The intuition is that any substring can be uniquely identified by its starting index i and ending index j (where i ≤ j). By systematically varying i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, varying j from i to n-1, we cover every possible substring exactly once. The outer loop controls the starting position, while the inner loop controls the ending position. This connects to L0 as it provides the substrings that will be checked for palindrome property. Time complexity is O(n²) for generating all substrings (there are n(n+1)/2 substrings). Space complexity is O(1) if we just use indices, or O(k) if we create substring objects. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, generating duplicate substrings, and inefficient substring creation. The real pattern involves clean loop boundaries and understanding the combinatorial nature of substring generation.

Algorithm:
1. For i from 0 to n-1:
2.   For j from i to n-1:
3.     Process substring s[i:j+1]

Time: O(n²) for generation (plus additional processing time)
Space: O(1) for indices, O(k) if creating substrings

examples.output: All substrings: a, ab, abc, b, bc, c
examples.trace:
String: "abc" (length 3)

i=0:
  j=0: substring s[0:1] = "a"
  j=1: substring s[0:2] = "ab"
  j=2: substring s[0:3] = "abc"

i=1:
  j=1: substring s[1:2] = "b"
  j=2: substring s[1:3] = "bc"

i=2:
  j=2: substring s[2:3] = "c"

Total: 6 substrings = 3*(3+1)/2 = 6

String: "ab" (length 2)

i=0:
  j=0: substring s[0:1] = "a"
  j=1: substring s[0:2] = "ab"

i=1:
  j=1: substring s[1:2] = "b"

Total: 3 substrings = 2*(2+1)/2 = 3

testCases.expected: 6 substrings: a, ab, abc, b, bc, c
10 substrings: a, ab, abc, abcd, b, bc, bcd, c, cd, d
6 substrings: a, ab, aba, b, ba, a
6 substrings: x, xy, xyz, y, yz, z
10 substrings: l, lo, lon, long, o, on, ong, n, ng, g

ladders.title: Building Block — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Understanding how to access characters and extract substrings using indices in programming languages.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept involves working with string indices to access individual characters and extract substrings. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters where each character has a position index starting from 0. String slicing allows extracting a contiguous portion of the string by specifying start and end indices. In most programming languages, s[i] gives the character at position i, while s[i:j] gives the substring from index i (inclusive) to j (exclusive). This connects to L2 as it's the mechanism used to generate substrings from the indices produced by nested loops. Time complexity for accessing a character is O(1), while slicing a substring of length k is O(k) as it creates a new string. Space complexity for slicing is O(k) for the new substring. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors with slice boundaries, confusing inclusive/exclusive indices, and assuming O(1) time for slicing. The real pattern involves understanding zero-based indexing and half-open intervals [i, j).

Algorithm:
1. Character access: char = s[index]
2. Substring extraction: substr = s[start:end]  # end is exclusive

Time: O(1) for character access, O(k) for substring extraction
Space: O(1) for character, O(k) for substring

examples.trace:
String: "hello" (indices: 0=h, 1=e, 2=l, 3=l, 4=o)

Character access s[1]:
- Index 1 corresponds to character 'e'
- Returns 'e'

Substring slice s[1:4]:
- Start index: 1 (inclusive) → 'e'
- End index: 4 (exclusive) → stops before index 4
- Characters included: indices 1, 2, 3 → 'e', 'l', 'l'
- Returns "ell"

String: "algorithm" (indices: 0=a, 1=l, 2=g, 3=o, 4=r, 5=i, 6=t, 7=h, 8=m)

Character access s[3]:
- Index 3 corresponds to character 'o'
- Returns 'o'

Substring slice s[0:5]:
- Start index: 0 (inclusive) → 'a'
- End index: 5 (exclusive) → stops before index 5
- Characters included: indices 0,1,2,3,4 → 'a','l','g','o','r'
- Returns "algor"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Characters in a String
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing two characters at specific positions in a string.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves accessing and comparing individual characters at given positions in a string. The intuition is that strings are composed of characters, and we can check if two characters are equal by comparing them directly. In programming, this is typically done using equality operators (== in most languages). The algorithm simply involves accessing the character at position i (s[i]) and the character at position j (s[j]), then checking if they are identical. This connects to L3 as it's the basic comparison used in string slicing validation and palindrome checking. Time complexity is O(1) for accessing and comparing two characters. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional storage is needed. Common mistakes include comparing string indices instead of characters, using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), and not handling cases where indices are out of bounds. The real pattern involves clean character access and proper comparison syntax.

Algorithm:
1. Access character at position i: char1 = s[i]
2. Access character at position j: char2 = s[j]
3. Compare: char1 == char2

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
String: "hello"
Position 1: s[1] = 'e'
Position 4: s[4] = 'o'
Compare: 'e' == 'o' → false

String: "racecar"
Position 0: s[0] = 'r'
Position 6: s[6] = 'r'
Compare: 'r' == 'r' → true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition and properties of palindromic strings.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: understanding what a palindrome is. A palindrome is a sequence (in this case, a string) that reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition is symmetry - the first character equals the last, the second equals the second-last, and so on. Examples include "racecar", "madam", "noon", and single characters like "a". An empty string is typically considered a palindrome by definition. This connects to L4 as it defines what we're trying to identify when comparing characters. There's no algorithm here, just conceptual understanding. Common mistakes include thinking palindromes must have odd length (false - "abba" is even), forgetting that single characters are palindromes, and not considering empty strings. The real pattern involves recognizing the symmetric property.

Key properties:
1. Symmetry: s[i] = s[n-1-i] for all i
2. Single characters are palindromes
3. Empty string is a palindrome
4. Can be odd or even length

Examples: "a", "aa", "aba", "abba", "racecar"
Non-examples: "ab", "abc", "hello"

examples.trace:
Read forwards: r-a-c-e-c-a-r
Read backwards: r-a-c-e-c-a-r
Both readings are identical → Palindrome
Check symmetry:
Position 0 (r) = Position 6 (r)
Position 1 (a) = Position 5 (a)
Position 2 (c) = Position 4 (c)
Position 3 (e) = Position 3 (e)
All symmetric pairs match

Read forwards: h-e-l-l-o
Read backwards: o-l-l-e-h
Readings differ → Not palindrome
Check symmetry:
Position 0 (h) ≠ Position 4 (o) → Mismatch found
No need to check further

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use a DP table to store palindrome status of substrings based on smaller ones. Build from base cases of length 1 and 2, then expand to longer substrings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Palindromic Substring using DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the DP approach to find the longest palindromic substring.
ladders.explanation: The DP approach solves the longest palindromic substring problem by building a 2D table dp[i][j] that indicates whether substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. The intuition is that a substring is a palindrome if its first and last characters match and the inner substring is also a palindrome. We start with base cases: single characters (always palindromes) and two-character substrings (palindromes if both characters match). For longer substrings (length > 2), we check dp[i+1][j-1] (the inner substring) and s[i] == s[j]. We track the longest palindrome found during this process. The algorithm initializes a table of size n×n, fills it for increasing substring lengths, and updates the longest palindrome start and length. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops over all substrings, and space complexity is O(n²) for the DP table. This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking logic as a subroutine. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors), not handling empty strings, and inefficient memory usage. Real code pattern: Initialize dp table, set base cases, iterate lengths from 3 to n, update dp and track longest, return substring.

examples.trace: s = 'babad', n=5. Initialize dp 5x5 table all False. Base cases: length 1: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][2]=True, dp[3][3]=True, dp[4][4]=True. Length 2: check s[0]==s[1]? 'b'=='a'? False, dp[0][1]=False; s[1]==s[2]? 'a'=='b'? False, dp[1][2]=False; s[2]==s[3]? 'b'=='a'? False, dp[2][3]=False; s[3]==s[4]? 'a'=='d'? False, dp[3][4]=False. Longest so far: start=0, max_len=1. Length 3: check i=0,j=2: s[0]=='b', s[2]=='b', match, dp[1][1]=True, so dp[0][2]=True, update start=0, max_len=3; i=1,j=3: s[1]=='a', s[3]=='a', match, dp[2][2]=True, dp[1][3]=True; i=2,j=4: s[2]=='b', s[4]=='d', no match, dp[2][4]=False. Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=='b', s[3]=='a', no match, dp[0][3]=False; i=1,j=4: s[1]=='a', s[4]=='d', no match, dp[1][4]=False. Length 5: i=0,j=4: s[0]=='b', s[4]=='d', no match, dp[0][4]=False. Final: start=0, max_len=3, return s[0:3]='bab'.

s = 'cbbd', n=4. Initialize dp 4x4 table all False. Base cases: length 1: all diagonal True. Length 2: check s[0]==s[1]? 'c'=='b'? False, dp[0][1]=False; s[1]==s[2]? 'b'=='b'? True, dp[1][2]=True, update start=1, max_len=2; s[2]==s[3]? 'b'=='d'? False, dp[2][3]=False. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=='c', s[2]=='b', no match, dp[0][2]=False; i=1,j=3: s[1]=='b', s[3]=='d', no match, dp[1][3]=False. Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=='c', s[3]=='d', no match, dp[0][3]=False. Final: start=1, max_len=2, return s[1:3]='bb'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Checking with DP Table
ladders.desc: Implement the core function to check if a substring is a palindrome using DP.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome by leveraging the DP table. The intuition is that we can determine palindrome status efficiently by building on previously computed smaller substrings. For a substring of length 1 or 2, we check directly: if length is 1, it's always a palindrome; if length is 2, check if the two characters match. For longer substrings, we check if the first and last characters match and if the inner substring (from i+1 to j-1) is already known to be a palindrome from the DP table. This avoids redundant checks and enables O(1) lookup after the table is built. The algorithm involves initializing or updating the DP table entry dp[i][j] based on these conditions. Time complexity for a single check is O(1) after table setup, but building the table requires O(n²) overall. Space complexity is O(n²) for the table. This connects to L0 as it's the essential logic used in the full solution to populate the DP table. Common mistakes include not handling base cases separately, incorrect indices for the inner substring, and assuming the DP table is filled in the correct order. Real code pattern: Function check_palindrome(i, j, s, dp): if j-i <= 1: return s[i] == s[j] if j-i == 1 else True; else return s[i] == s[j] and dp[i+1][j-1].

examples.trace: Given s='aba', i=0, j=2. Length = j-i+1 = 3 > 2, so check s[0]=='a' and s[2]=='a': match. Then check inner substring s[1:2] which is 'b', length 1, always palindrome. Thus, dp[1][1] should be True (from base case). Return True.

Given s='abca', i=0, j=3. Length = 4 > 2, check s[0]=='a' and s[3]=='a': match. Then check inner substring s[1:3]='bc', length 2, need dp[1][2]. For 'bc', s[1]=='b', s[2]=='c', no match, so dp[1][2]=False. Thus, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Building DP Table for Palindrome Detection
ladders.desc: Construct the DP table to store palindrome status for all substrings.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves building a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] is True if substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome. The intuition is to use dynamic programming to avoid recomputation: a substring is a palindrome if its ends match and the inner part is a palindrome. We fill the table by increasing substring length. Start with base cases: all single-character substrings (dp[i][i] = True) and two-character substrings (dp[i][i+1] = True if s[i] == s[i+1]). Then, for lengths from 3 to n, for each start i, compute j = i + length - 1, and set dp[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j] and dp[i+1][j-1]). This ensures we only check each substring once, leveraging previously computed values. Time complexity is O(n²) as we iterate over O(n²) substrings, each check is O(1). Space complexity is O(n²) for the table. This connects to L1 as it provides the table used in the palindrome checking subroutine. Common mistakes include filling the table in wrong order (must fill by increasing length), not initializing base cases correctly, and memory inefficiency. Real code pattern: Initialize n x n dp table with False; for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = True; for i in range(n-1): if s[i]==s[i+1]: dp[i][i+1]=True; for length in range(3, n+1): for i in range(n-length+1): j = i+length-1; if s[i]==s[j] and dp[i+1][j-1]: dp[i][j]=True.

examples.trace: s='abba', n=4. Initialize dp 4x4 all False. Base cases: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][2]=True, dp[3][3]=True. Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=='a', s[1]=='b', no match, dp[0][1]=False; i=1,j=2: s[1]=='b', s[2]=='b', match, dp[1][2]=True; i=2,j=3: s[2]=='b', s[3]=='a', no match, dp[2][3]=False. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=='a', s[2]=='b', no match, dp[0][2]=False; i=1,j=3: s[1]=='b', s[3]=='a', no match, dp[1][3]=False. Length 4: i=0,j=3: s[0]=='a', s[3]=='a', match, check dp[1][2]=True, so dp[0][3]=True. Final dp: rows 0-3, cols 0-3 with True at (0,0),(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(0,3).

s='abc', n=3. Initialize dp 3x3 all False. Base cases: dp[0][0]=True, dp[1][1]=True, dp[2][2]=True. Length 2: i=0,j=1: s[0]=='a', s[1]=='b', no match, dp[0][1]=False; i=1,j=2: s[1]=='b', s[2]=='c', no match, dp[1][2]=False. Length 3: i=0,j=2: s[0]=='a', s[2]=='c', no match, dp[0][2]=False. Final dp: only diagonal True.

testCases.expected: Table with True for all palindromic substrings

ladders.title: Building Block — Substring Palindrome Base Cases
ladders.desc: Identify and handle base cases for palindrome detection: single characters and two-character strings.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the simplest cases for determining if a substring is a palindrome: substrings of length 1 and 2. The intuition is that these are the foundational elements from which longer palindromes are built. For a substring of length 1 (i.e., a single character), it is always a palindrome because it reads the same forwards and backwards. For a substring of length 2, it is a palindrome only if both characters are identical. These base cases are critical because they serve as the starting point for dynamic programming: we initialize the DP table with these values before expanding to longer substrings. The algorithm involves checking the indices i and j: if i == j, it's length 1, return True; if j == i+1, it's length 2, return s[i] == s[j]. Time complexity is O(1) per check, and space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as these base cases are used to initialize the DP table. Common mistakes include not handling empty substrings (not needed here as i and j are valid indices), confusing length calculations, and missing the length 2 case. Real code pattern: if i == j: return True; elif j == i+1: return s[i] == s[j]; else: proceed to longer cases.

examples.trace: Given s='hello', i=1, j=1. Since i == j, substring is s[1:2] = 'e', length 1. A single character is always a palindrome, so return True.
Given s='hello', i=1, j=2. Here, j = i+1, so substring is s[1:3] = 'el', length 2. Check if s[1] == s[2]: 'e' == 'l'? False, so return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison for Palindrome Check
ladders.desc: Compare characters at the start and end of a substring to determine potential palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two characters in a string to see if they are equal, which is a fundamental step in checking for palindromes. The intuition is that for any substring to be a palindrome, the first and last characters must match; if they don't, the substring cannot be a palindrome regardless of the inner characters. This operation is performed by accessing the string at indices i and j and using the equality operator (==). It's a simple O(1) time operation with no extra space. In the context of the DP approach, this comparison is used in both base cases (for length 2 substrings) and for longer substrings (where we also check the inner substring). This connects to L3 as it's part of the logic for handling base cases and extending to longer substrings. Common mistakes include using incorrect indices, not handling string bounds, and confusing character comparison with substring extraction. Real code pattern: if s[i] == s[j]: proceed to check inner part; else: not a palindrome.

examples.trace: Given s='racecar', i=0, j=6. Compare s[0] and s[6]: s[0] = 'r', s[6] = 'r'. Since 'r' == 'r', they match, so return True for this comparison step.
Given s='python', i=0, j=5. Compare s[0] and s[5]: s[0] = 'p', s[5] = 'n'. Since 'p' != 'n', they do not match, so return False for this comparison step.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromes in strings.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence of characters that reads the same forwards and backwards. In the context of strings, this means that the string is identical when reversed. For example, 'racecar' reversed is 'racecar', so it's a palindrome. The intuition is that palindromes have symmetry around their center. Key properties: single characters are always palindromes; empty strings are often considered palindromes but may not be relevant in this problem. To check if a string is a palindrome, you can compare it to its reverse or use two pointers starting from the ends and moving inward, comparing characters. This foundational concept is essential because the entire problem revolves around identifying such substrings. Time complexity for checking a string of length m is O(m) if comparing to reverse or using two pointers. Space complexity is O(1) for two-pointer approach. This connects to L4 as character comparison is a basic operation in palindrome checking. Common mistakes include ignoring case sensitivity (in this problem, assume case-sensitive), not handling odd/even lengths correctly, and confusing substrings with subsequences. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): return s == s[::-1] or use two pointers.

examples.trace: String: 'madam'. Reverse it: 'madam'. Since original equals reverse, it is a palindrome. Alternatively, use two pointers: compare 'm' and 'm' (match), then 'a' and 'a' (match), then 'd' and 'd' (match). All match, so True.
String: 'hello'. Reverse it: 'olleh'. Since 'hello' != 'olleh', it is not a palindrome. Alternatively, two pointers: compare 'h' and 'o' (no match), so False immediately.

approaches.name: Expand Around Center O(n²)
approaches.summary: Treat each character and gap as a center, expand outward to find palindromes. Handle odd and even lengths separately by checking left and right characters.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Palindromic Substring using Expand Around Center
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding the longest palindromic substring by expanding around all possible centers.
ladders.explanation: The Expand Around Center approach solves the longest palindromic substring problem by treating each character and each gap between characters as potential centers of palindromes. The intuition is that any palindrome has a center point (or two center points for even-length palindromes), and we can find all palindromes by expanding outward from these centers until the characters no longer match. This approach works because it systematically explores all possible palindrome centers in the string, ensuring we don't miss any candidate palindromes. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize variables to track the start and end indices of the longest palindrome found. 2) For each position i in the string (0 to n-1), treat it as a center for odd-length palindromes and expand outward. 3) For each position i (0 to n-2), treat the gap between i and i+1 as a center for even-length palindromes and expand outward. 4) During expansion, check if left and right characters match; if they do, continue expanding; if not, stop and update the longest palindrome if the current one is longer. 5) Return the substring from start to end indices. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of the 2n-1 possible centers (n characters + n-1 gaps), we might expand up to O(n) times in the worst case (e.g., string of all same characters). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the parent problem by being the complete implementation of the Expand Around Center approach. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle even-length palindromes (only checking odd centers), not updating indices correctly when a longer palindrome is found, and off-by-one errors in expansion bounds. Real code pattern: def longestPalindrome(s): n = len(s); if n < 2: return s; start, end = 0, 0; for i in range(n): len1 = expand(s, i, i); len2 = expand(s, i, i+1); max_len = max(len1, len2); if max_len > end - start: start = i - (max_len - 1) // 2; end = i + max_len // 2; return s[start:end+1]; def expand(s, left, right): while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]: left -= 1; right += 1; return right - left - 1.

examples.trace: String: 'babad', n=5. Initialize start=0, end=0. i=0 (center 'b'): Expand odd: left=0, right=0, s[0]=='b' matches, expand left=-1 (out of bounds), stop, length=1. Expand even: left=0, right=1, s[0]=='b', s[1]=='a', no match, length=0. max_len=1, not longer than current (end-start=0), no update. i=1 (center 'a'): Expand odd: left=1, right=1, match 'a', expand left=0, right=2, s[0]=='b', s[2]=='b', match, expand left=-1, right=3 (out of bounds), stop, length=3. Expand even: left=1, right=2, s[1]=='a', s[2]=='b', no match, length=0. max_len=3, longer than current, update start=1-(3-1)//2=0, end=1+3//2=2. i=2 (center 'b'): Expand odd: left=2, right=2, match 'b', expand left=1, right=3, s[1]=='a', s[3]=='a', match, expand left=0, right=4, s[0]=='b', s[4]=='d', no match, stop, length=3. Expand even: left=2, right=3, s[2]=='b', s[3]=='a', no match, length=0. max_len=3, not longer than current (length 3 same), no update. i=3 (center 'a'): Expand odd: left=3, right=3, match 'a', expand left=2, right=4, s[2]=='b', s[4]=='d', no match, length=1. Expand even: left=3, right=4, s[3]=='a', s[4]=='d', no match, length=0. max_len=1, no update. i=4 (center 'd'): Expand odd: left=4, right=4, match 'd', length=1. Expand even: left=4, right=5 (out of bounds), length=0. max_len=1, no update. Final start=0, end=2, substring 'bab'.

String: 'cbbd', n=4. Initialize start=0, end=0. i=0 (center 'c'): Expand odd: left=0, right=0, match 'c', length=1. Expand even: left=0, right=1, s[0]=='c', s[1]=='b', no match, length=0. max_len=1, update start=0, end=0. i=1 (center 'b'): Expand odd: left=1, right=1, match 'b', expand left=0, right=2, s[0]=='c', s[2]=='b', no match, stop, length=1. Expand even: left=1, right=2, s[1]=='b', s[2]=='b', match, expand left=0, right=3, s[0]=='c', s[3]=='d', no match, stop, length=2. max_len=2, longer than current, update start=1-(2-1)//2=1, end=1+2//2=2. i=2 (center 'b'): Expand odd: left=2, right=2, match 'b', expand left=1, right=3, s[1]=='b', s[3]=='d', no match, length=1. Expand even: left=2, right=3, s[2]=='b', s[3]=='d', no match, length=0. max_len=1, no update. i=3 (center 'd'): Expand odd: left=3, right=3, match 'd', length=1. Expand even: left=3, right=4 (out of bounds), length=0. max_len=1, no update. Final start=1, end=2, substring 'bb'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Expand Function for Palindrome Detection
ladders.desc: Helper function that expands from a given center to find the longest palindrome.
ladders.explanation: The expand function is the core sub-routine in the Expand Around Center approach, responsible for detecting the longest palindrome centered at a given position or gap. The intuition is that given a center (which could be a single character for odd-length palindromes or a gap between characters for even-length palindromes), we can find the maximum palindrome by expanding outward symmetrically until the characters no longer match. This works because palindromes are symmetric around their center, so checking left and right characters incrementally ensures we capture the entire palindrome. The algorithm: 1) Take input string s, left index, and right index. For odd-length palindromes, left and right start at the same index (the center character). For even-length palindromes, left and right start adjacent (representing the gap center). 2) While left index is >= 0 (within string start) and right index is < len(s) (within string end) and s[left] equals s[right], decrement left by 1 and increment right by 1 to expand outward. 3) When the loop stops (due to mismatch or out of bounds), return the length of the palindrome found, calculated as (right - left - 1). This subtraction accounts for the final increment/decrement that broke the loop. Time complexity for this function is O(k) where k is the length of the palindrome found, but in the context of the full algorithm, it contributes to O(n²) overall. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the parent problem by being called for each possible center to find candidate palindromes. Common mistakes include: not checking bounds before accessing s[left] and s[right] (leading to index errors), incorrect length calculation (should be right-left-1, not right-left+1), and misunderstanding center representation for even palindromes. Real code pattern: def expand(s, left, right): while left >= 0 and right < len(s) and s[left] == s[right]: left -= 1; right += 1; return right - left - 1.

examples.trace: String: 'racecar', left=3, right=3 (center 'e'). Initial: left=3, right=3, s[3]=='e' matches itself. Expand: left=2, right=4, s[2]=='c', s[4]=='c', match. Expand: left=1, right=5, s[1]=='a', s[5]=='a', match. Expand: left=0, right=6, s[0]=='r', s[6]=='r', match. Expand: left=-1, right=7, out of bounds, stop loop. Length = right - left - 1 = 7 - (-1) - 1 = 7.

String: 'abba', left=1, right=2 (gap between 'b' and 'b'). Initial: left=1, right=2, s[1]=='b', s[2]=='b', match. Expand: left=0, right=3, s[0]=='a', s[3]=='a', match. Expand: left=-1, right=4, out of bounds, stop loop. Length = right - left - 1 = 4 - (-1) - 1 = 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Odd and Even Palindrome Centers
ladders.desc: Technique of treating characters and gaps as centers to capture all palindrome possibilities.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the Expand Around Center approach is the insight that palindromes can be categorized into odd-length and even-length, each requiring different center representations. For odd-length palindromes (e.g., 'aba', length 3), the center is a single character at a specific index. For even-length palindromes (e.g., 'abba', length 4), the center is the gap between two characters. By considering both types of centers, we ensure that all possible palindromes in the string are explored. The intuition is that any substring palindrome must have a definable center: if the length is odd, the center is at index i; if even, the center is between indices i and i+1. This works because it covers all symmetric points in the string. The algorithm involves iterating through all possible centers: for i from 0 to n-1, check odd centers (i,i); for i from 0 to n-2, check even centers (i,i+1). This results in 2n-1 total centers (n odd + (n-1) even). For each center, we expand outward using the expand function to find the longest palindrome at that center. This logic is central because it transforms the problem from checking all substrings (O(n³)) to checking centers (O(n²)). Time complexity for this logic alone is O(n) for iterating centers, but when combined with expansion, it becomes O(n²). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the main loop that calls the expand function. Common mistakes include: only checking odd centers (missing even-length palindromes), incorrect iteration bounds for even centers (should go to n-2), and confusion about center indices. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): odd_len = expand(s, i, i); even_len = expand(s, i, i+1); max_len = max(odd_len, even_len); update longest palindrome if max_len is larger.

examples.output: Centers: odd at i=0,1,2; even at i=0,1
examples.trace: String 'aba', n=3. Odd centers: i=0 (center 'a'), i=1 (center 'b'), i=2 (center 'a'). Even centers: i=0 (gap between 'a' and 'b'), i=1 (gap between 'b' and 'a'). For each center, expand function is called to find palindrome length. This ensures all palindromes are checked: odd-length 'a' at i=0, odd-length 'aba' at i=1, odd-length 'a' at i=2, even-length none at i=0, even-length none at i=1.

examples.output: Centers: odd at i=0,1,2,3; even at i=0,1,2
examples.trace: String 'abba', n=4. Odd centers: i=0 ('a'), i=1 ('b'), i=2 ('b'), i=3 ('a'). Even centers: i=0 (gap 'a'-'b'), i=1 (gap 'b'-'b'), i=2 (gap 'b'-'a'). Expanding from even center i=1 finds palindrome 'abba'. This shows even centers are crucial for capturing even-length palindromes.

ladders.title: Building Block — Palindrome Symmetry Check
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of verifying if a substring is a palindrome by comparing symmetric characters.
ladders.explanation: Palindrome symmetry check is the building block for determining if a given substring is a palindrome, which is essential for the expansion process. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, meaning it has symmetric characters around its center. The intuition is that by comparing characters from the ends towards the center, we can verify symmetry efficiently. For a substring from index l to r (inclusive), it is a palindrome if for all i from 0 to (r-l)//2, s[l+i] equals s[r-i]. This works because it checks each pair of symmetric positions. The algorithm: 1) Initialize two pointers, left at l and right at r. 2) While left < right, compare s[left] and s[right]. If they are not equal, return false (not a palindrome). 3) Increment left and decrement right. 4) If loop completes without mismatch, return true (palindrome). Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the substring (r-l+1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the fundamental check used during expansion in the expand function. Common mistakes include: using left <= right as loop condition (which can cause unnecessary comparison at center for odd-length), off-by-one errors in indices, and not handling empty or single-character substrings correctly (they are trivially palindromes). Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s, l, r): while l < r: if s[l] != s[r]: return False; l += 1; r -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Substring 'racecar' from l=0 to r=6. Initial: left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r'), match. Increment left=1, decrement right=5. left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a'), match. left=2, right=4. left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c'), match. left=3, right=3, loop condition left<r is false (3<3 false), exit loop. All comparisons matched, return True.
Substring 'ell' from l=1 to r=3. Initial: left=1 ('e'), right=3 ('l'), no match, return False immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing two characters in a string for equality.
ladders.explanation: String character comparison is the basic operation used throughout the palindrome detection process. It involves checking if two characters at specific indices in a string are equal. The intuition is simple: characters are the building blocks of strings, and palindrome symmetry relies on comparing these characters pairwise. This works because strings are sequences of characters, and accessing them by index allows direct comparison. The algorithm: given a string s and two indices i and j, return true if s[i] equals s[j], false otherwise. This requires ensuring i and j are within the valid range of indices (0 to len(s)-1) to avoid index errors. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the core operation in palindrome symmetry checks; each comparison in the symmetry check uses this basic operation. Common mistakes include: comparing indices instead of characters (e.g., i == j instead of s[i] == s[j]), not validating indices before access, and misunderstanding character equality (case-sensitive in most contexts). Real code pattern: if i >= 0 and j < len(s) and s[i] == s[j]: return True else False.

examples.trace: String 'hello', indices i=1, j=3. Check bounds: i=1 (within 0 to 4), j=3 (within 0 to 4). Access s[1] = 'e', s[3] = 'l'. Compare 'e' == 'l'? False, so return False.
String 'world', indices i=2, j=2. Check bounds: both within 0 to 4. Access s[2] = 'r', s[2] = 'r'. Compare 'r' == 'r'? True, so return True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: understanding palindromes as strings that read the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a fundamental concept in string processing, defined as a sequence of characters that reads the same forwards and backwards. Examples include single characters ('a'), words ('racecar', 'noon'), or phrases ('madam'). The intuition is symmetry: if you reverse the string, it remains identical to the original. This works because each character has a matching counterpart at the symmetric position from the opposite end. For instance, in 'aba', the first 'a' matches the last 'a', and the middle 'b' is the center. Palindromes can be odd-length (like 'aba' with a center character) or even-length (like 'abba' with a center gap). Understanding this is crucial for the Expand Around Center approach, as it relies on identifying centers and expanding symmetrically. To check if a string is a palindrome, you can compare characters from both ends moving inward: start with left=0 and right=len(s)-1, compare s[left] and s[right], if equal, move left++ and right--, continue until left >= right. If all comparisons match, it's a palindrome. Time complexity for checking is O(n) where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the basis for character comparison operations. Common mistakes include: confusing palindromes with other patterns (e.g., anagrams), not considering empty strings as palindromes (they are, by definition), and case sensitivity (e.g., 'Racecar' is not a palindrome if case matters). Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: String 'aba'. Left=0 ('a'), right=2 ('a'), match. Move left=1, right=1. Left < right? 1<1 false, exit loop. All characters matched, so it's a palindrome.
String 'abc'. Left=0 ('a'), right=2 ('c'), no match, return False immediately.
